video_title: Denny Hamlin: The truth about competing against his own team
transcript: Welcome to a new episode of Power Players with Brian Sozzi. I am your host Brian Sozzi and you all know that I love me the Yahoo brand. I love Yahoo Finance where I work. I love me some Yahoo Sports, Yahoo News, Yahoo Mail, and even our new Yahoo Scout AI. So I was I was jacked up, full stop. I was jacked up when I saw the purple number 11 Yahoo car cross the finish line, first place earlier this year. And of that course, that car is driven by Denny Hamlin. And I am so psyched that that Denny is here to talk about all things racing. I know but yes, man. I'm holding I'm holding the winning car here, man. Like this is like a real thing. Thank you for doing this. I appreciate it. >> Of course. Yeah, it was awesome day to to win that race there in Michigan. First my very first win with Yahoo on the car, which is fantastic. >> I love it. I So I saw the car cross, you came out. I'm very jealous of your white and purple Yahoo suit. Frankly, I should be in that thing hosting all of my shows there. But then But then the whole crew, Denny. I mean, they're wearing the outfit, too. I'm like, this is just I mean, this is just amazing. But thank you again for doing this. You know, just Let's start on that win because I think there are like multiple chapters to the past 12 months in your life that you're that you're having. Let's start on you the the racer. What was it like to get that win? >> Yeah, I mean, just you know, every win is critical, but you know, when you're getting to the the tail end of my career. I'm not you know, I'm not at the beginning, not in the middle. I'm definitely towards the end. Like every win becomes a little bit more special cuz you just never know when it's your last. But you know, we we've just been on a tear over the last few years, especially you know, the last two or three, just winning a lot of races. And you know, winning up in Michigan was was fantastic for us and I tell you it just has been a great role we've been on. >> Don't tell me you're you're not retiring. All right, I I I need to see that purple car going through the finish line again. I mean, don't even hit me with that. It's too much. >> I know. I know it has to happen and you know, certainly it's going to happen sooner or later. I I I've already had the discussions with Joe Gibbs Racing. They're already asking me, "Are you sure you want to stop?" You know, you everything's going so well right now. I said, "Just get with me in a few months. We'll talk again." >> How is the How is the preparation, you know, these days at this stage of your career different to when you started? >> It's It's just so much more analytical now. I mean, just like, you know, with technology and how, you know, probably companies run their business nowadays, it's dramatically different than it used to be. It used to be, you know, off the cuff and you you had to go out there and you had to dig into all the the numbers to find out, you know, where you need to improve and it's in racing is the same way. I mean, I really embraced analytics probably I see, I went through a winless season in 2018 and then at that time the teams started doing some analytics and data post race to kind of analyze how the driver did, the car did, things like that. Around 2018-19, I'm like, you know, I'm going to embrace this and figure out where my weaknesses are. So, I basically said, "All right, crew chief, tell me all the areas in which you think I can improve on and what does the data say? Does it back up what I feel is correct, what you think is correct?" And I just started embracing it and then every off season I get my list of five major things that as a driver I need to improve on that the data says and I go to work on those things and I've tried to find speed in those areas and I just felt like I've just kept getting better and better ever since. >> What were those five things coming into this year that you wanted to address? And I I can't imagine just it's not just like the the actual car, but like it does it change how you you train off of the track? >> Um it it changes how I look at data week to week. So, um I'm always analyzing what happened the last time I was at that particular track. You know, if I'm going into this weekend at Texas, um so I'm I'm looking back at the previous probably two to three Texas races, looking at trends, how did I run, why did I not finish to my potential on that day. The five key things I would say coming into this year were I need to score more points on road courses, I need to score more points on super speedways, I've got to improve my restarts, I've got to improve um my defensive rating, which essentially is when a car approaches, can I hold them off for a longer period of time before they actually pass me. Um and then overall, it's just improving stage points. And so, those are the key things that I feel like certainly three of those five we definitely started the year in a better position statistically than what I've been in the past. >> You know, what um you know, have you changed as a driver? >> You know, I feel like you just never can be too old to learn new things. And even though I've been in the sport for 21 years, and >> believe it, 21 years. I still remember when you came in. >> I I >> Oh my god. >> It um you know, you would think that I know all the cracks and crevices of these racetracks and how to approach them and all that, but I I go into every weekend preparing as if I've never been. And I just make sure that I put the work in, and because I mean, listen, that's the younger guys, and just like any other sport, the athletes today are better than the ones from 10 years ago. They they've got more training, more more um uh they start younger. And so, it's hard for me to keep up with the young guys in the speed category. I just have to use my experience to beat them mentally week and week out. >> You know what is amazing? You are Well, not amazing. I mean, you said you're as at the time of taping this, you're in what? Second place in the in the point standings. So, how do you like what do you need to do to to get to one? Like what I mean, outside of like I mean, I kind of have a sense of where you may go, but maybe I'm wrong. >> Well, what's very interesting in our sport is that uh the the the car that's number one is the team that I own. And so, um I uh very advantageous for them, but they have a car owner that they actually that actually competes on the track with them. So, I go into their meetings every Monday and say, "Well, here's what I experienced. Here's what I think you should be looking out for next time." And so, I've got these young drivers that are wildly talented. I I hired them for a reason. And then I give them my all my tips and tricks out of my journals from years and years, and they take it and they run with it, and they win with it. And so, it's very unique that, you know, I go and compete against the cars that I own, but you know, I'm associated with the top two in the standings right now. So, I I'm proud of that. >> Uh would it be hard or so, we should Number one, we should say, of course, is uh Tyler Reddick. I and your team is 2311, uh which you also co-own with Michael Jordan. So, like that was the other chapter to your life like that I mentioned here at the top. Um would it be hard to beat the person that you signed to your team? Or you're on the track, you just want to beat their ass. Like it is what it is. >> No, I I root for them to finish second. That's what I really want. I'm selfish. I I want to win. That the best case scenario for me is I win, they finish second. Um similar because, listen, I know my career is is coming to an end. So, I want to accomplish all the individual goals that I have as as a as a driver now because I I don't I'm never going to have another chance to doing that. My team will be racing for decades long after I'm done racing. So, they have plenty of time to accomplish all the championships and the wins and everything. So, I of course selfishly want to win for my team, my Yahoo team, my number 11 team uh to get those wins and stack up you know, where we stand in the in the all-time win list. >> I mean, you really have some some modern-day heavyweights on that team. I mean, Tyler Reddick, I mean, Bubba Wallace. What did you see in these young drivers that that made you want to say, you know what? They need to be the foundation of my team. >> You know, there's just things that I can tell that you can't see on TV. Um, it's just professionals in certain sports can see when other competitors are better than maybe their equipment that they're currently in. Or, you know what? If they knew what I knew or if they were in the car that I was in, they would really succeed. And so, I can just tell by some of the moves that they make that hey, that's pretty difficult to do and that's hard to pull off especially with the car that they're probably driving at the moment. So, I just can I can see it. I can I can peep against it so I know when I see something exceptional out of certain drivers. >> How do you explain I mean, to the casual watcher out there um, you know, the the run of excellence that Tyler has been on and how I look, I've never I've never seen anything like this before. I mean, how do you explain it? >> It's very very hard to it it's an anomaly. I mean, we were going over statistics yesterday and I'm on pace right with where the leader of the points, the number one position has been for the last five to six years. I've I've scored the same amount of points. But there's one car that is way above the curve and winning more than the numbers show it should. And so it's just it's an anomaly and and it you know, eventually it works itself out, but I'm I'm happy it's them and not somebody else. >> But I the car is a pretty they're pretty equal in terms of setup, right? So I mean there's a real this is Tyler. Yeah, I mean this is essentially the human component just I mean taking over and and driving a season and no pun intended like into the record books. >> Absolutely and and yes, NASCAR went through a shift probably four years ago of going to a more homologated car to where everyone's driving very similar cars. Now they're all not exactly the same, but they're similar. So the idea was that the drivers will make the difference of your performance each and every weekend. So yes, you would say that Tyler's being is that X factor right now that is you know, creating this result and he's doing it in wild ways. I mean he's getting in wrecks and the team is finding ways to repair the car and still goes on to win and it's again, it's very very unique situation that we haven't seen in some time. >> How do you have you know, as a leader and and some as a team owner or someone out still out there racing, but someone who has seen so much over their career, how do you manage his expectations over the next few years? I mean this is you just mentioned I mean this is an anomaly season. I mean it's mind-blowing. I mean how do you how do you manage those inevitable downs? >> Yeah, you're going to have them. I mean there's no you can't sustain like I think he's got like a 5.0 average finish right now. That is not sustainable. It's just not in our sport. There's too many variables that are kind of out of your control. You can get caught up in someone else's wreck. There's too many things that make that sustainable. So, week in week out, I always encourage those guys to find goals that, you know, you will be happy leaving the racetrack with each and every week because in our sport, if you can win 7% of the time, you're a Hall of Famer. So, he's on a current like 60% win streak, right? It's just silly. >> [laughter] >> Yeah, so it it's you have to you're going to have to find happiness in something other than winning every week because you're going to lose far more than you're going to win. It's still It's This is not a 1v1 uh sport that, you know, you're you're judged on your win-loss record. You will win You will lose far more than you're going to win. So, it's finding those mini goals of every week "Hey, I improved at this racetrack. I know the result wasn't great, but I know I performed better here than I had in the years past." >> Is it hard to keep talent like this under your roof uh for longer term in terms of like a deal structure? >> Yeah, I mean, certainly. It's just It's just like any other sport. When someone performs at a very high level, when it comes contract time, it's "Well, I I deserve to be, you know, one of the highest paid." So, that's the balance that you have and and it all works itself out and you know, we just got our deal done with Tyler for for the next, you know, foreseeable future, really. And um you know, he's the guy that I I picked out four years ago as, you know, wanting to be that franchise-changing driver and he's he's done it, he's proved it and he got paid accordingly. >> How long do these deals last? Is it the same as baseball? It's like 6 to 8 years? >> It just kind of depends. Um it's a little bit more short-term than that. I think, you know, some drivers are on one-year deals. Just one-year deal. Just like any NBA players or whatever that, you know, it just depends on where you're at in the series. Um, >> [clears throat] >> and some deals can go as long as 4 to 6 years. So, it's just it just depends on, you know, where the team is financially and, you know, how much they believe in the driver. >> Clearly, I you have a knack for building teams, like successful teams. Do you want to add more drivers to your team? >> Well, we got, you know, four really good drivers right now. We've got some young guys like Corey Heim and that are development drivers that I personally believe will be um, the next Tyler Reddick. Um, you know, it just it takes time to to bring these guys in and train them and get them used to racing at the top level. So, uh, I feel very confident with, you know, what we've got and where we're heading towards the future. So, um, the the pathway towards the top for 2311 is, I think, a very clear path. >> Would you would you do your I don't even know if you can do this, but you do your final season under your own brand? Is that even possible? >> It It's possible. Um, I would have to race open, which, you know, probably zero listeners know what that is that tend to watch this, but which means I would have to race an un- uh, chartered car, which means it's an extra franchise that doesn't count as a franchise. So, we're limited to only having four cars on the racetrack that are part of, um, that part of is part of the NASCAR pay structure. So, they they pay a certain amount to the franchise teams. There's only 36 of them. So, we we could enter a fourth car for myself, but it would be we wouldn't get the payout that all the other cars get. Now, we would be allowed to compete, go for race wins and all that stuff, and win the prize money, but it would financially not be advantageous to us to do that. And then, you know, the other part of that is I I now I'm racing for my team, which is great, but I'd be racing an expansion team. Like, the people wouldn't be as experienced as what I'm currently with with Joe Gibbs Racing. Um, I'm, you know, with one of the top teams, one of the top teams in the top team of Joe Gibbs Racing right now. So, you know, I want to be able to, you know, be good enough to win my final race. And to do that, you got to be with a you know, everyone's got to be at top of their game. And it just would be really hard to do that with an an expansion team of my own. >> What, from what I could tell, you've had exposure to really two titans uh, in sports. One, Michael Jordan, who was your partner at 23 XI. I want to get to him in a second. But, Joe Gibbs, um, what have you learned from the from about like ownership, from being in that environment for so long? >> Well, I think that I modeled how we built our team a lot from Joe Gibbs and and how he runs his team. I mean, the guy is an absolute workhorse. Uh, he's he's over 80 years old and he's at that shop every day walking the floor, asking the people, "What do you need for us to win more races?" You know, going through every department, trying to figure out how they can win more races. And so, um, I tried to lead by example because, you know, I I've been part of an organization where it you know, this is a family organization in Joe Gibbs Racing. It's all they do. Um, I I don't envision myself working as hard as Joe is at his age. But, um, he knows how to put the right people in the right place to succeed. That's how he became a Hall of Famer in multiple sports, which very few people have. >> No, that's so true. And then, also in the Hall of Fame is Michael Jordan. Like, for those not familiar, how did this relationship even come about? >> Yeah, I was a season ticket holder at the Hornets. Um, he saw me walking by during halftime. He had security pull me aside, said Michael wanted to have a conversation. I was, of course, saying quite starstruck. So, he pulled me over, we started talking, and he's asking, you know, why did I only take two tires last weekend instead of staying out? And I'm like, wait a minute, what's going on? Like, you watch NASCAR racing? And he's like, oh man, I love it. I I never miss, every Sunday. So, that, of course, starts, you know, you exchange numbers, you have text conversations, I go down there, play golf with him a few times. So, we just started our friendship about 15, 16 years ago. And you know, it just steadily progressed itself to then COVID happens. I had interest in starting a race team. I needed a partner. Brought the idea to him. He's He said he's in. >> You know, I see all these photos, Denny, of him on the track. He's got the headphones on. Like, what is What is he telling the team in these high-pressure moments? I Is he just like just sending out magic dust? Like, the the Jordan magic dust over the place. God, I mean, the guy's a The guy's a freaking winner. I mean, what I What can you say about Michael Jordan? >> Yeah, he's been pretty disciplined about just listening and not actually keying the mic into the radio. He could He could if he wanted to. But now, he he's just He's a big fan of the sport. And I can't tell you what it does for our personnel that are in the pits with him, right? He's sitting in their pit box, um high-fiving the pit crews after they do a pit stop. Like, it's really motivating for them to see him and see how, you know, they have the ability, and I told them in the off-season, you guys have the ability to bring joy to the most famous athlete in the entire world. Like, that You control that power. So, what you do with that is very, very important. And so, they took it to heart, and they've obviously started this year on absolute fire. >> You know, I think you and I are around the same age. We both probably grew up watching Jordan play every single day. We need to watch him all these YouTube videos. But key to his success, every year he found new ways to get incrementally better, and that made him better than everybody else. What has he told you about, you know, planning for next season and the season after that? Like, how do you see the team evolving, in part because he is so competitive and he wants to win every single race? >> Well, the biggest thing I've noticed is that, you know, we have we hold off-season meetings and we we kind of are very honest with each other about where our team is. And this one was a really good off-season chat, and we had Michael sit in on it and give his perspective, and he says, you know, "The main thing I'm listening for and looking for, week in, week out, is that I want to know from all of our leadership and all of our management, all of our drivers, tell me, week in, week out, when you're debriefing with your team on what y'all could do better, talk about what you could do better. You You might think that I've just done an amazing job and it's their fault they didn't tighten the tires enough, or whatever it might be." He's like, "For every, you know, think about when you're pointing, you got three fingers pointing back at you. And so, never point the finger at someone else. Um, always be the one to self-reflect and and figure out what you can do better, and everyone has a very crucial role in our performance. And so, always analyze uh yourself and what you're in charge of. And and I think that that really was beneficial because, you know, it's very easy in our sport, that is a team sport. I know the drivers get all the recognition, but it is a team sport about, you know, every person, um, you know, really being honest with themselves. And you get the best out of each person and and their particular job, you're going to have a successful team. >> Is it fair to say his winning attitude, and look, your winning attitude, too. I mean, you you've crushed it for many years. Like, that has that has infiltrated every part of the 23XI organization. Cuz I mean, you just brought up that the team is managing to fix these cars quicker than before, get the team back on the track, and you're still winning races. Like, that is not those aren't things necessarily that you have seen in the past in racing. >> I think that they've tasted excellence, and now they're just feeding off of it. It and and and so, I think that a lot of thing two things can happen. When you find success, you either can get complacent with that success, or you can it feeds you to want to go get more. And I've seen with 2311, it's feeding those guys to go get more. Now, they're like hungry to say, "Oh, I know we're doing great things, but like we want to be greater yet." And so, that's what I really like about it. And I think that Michael's really helped with that mindset. >> I just give it all the winning you've had this year, like how has that changed the the sponsorship dynamic? The only comparison I can really make to is, you know, look at the McLaren team in F1. They have now they have sponsors for for golf bags. >> I mean, there is just I everywhere I look, everywhere I look, there's something McLaren orange all over the place. Like, how has all this winning changed your team? Well, what I've actually learned when I talk about all the things that I've learned from Joe Gibbs is that um back when I started this race [clears throat] team, the first thing he says is, "You you're crazy. This is a terrible business to be starting." He said, "But one thing I can I can certainly tell you is that winning fixes everything, and especially on the commercial side, right? And so, when you go out there and and you're as dominant as Tyler Reddick is is been, certainly on the commercial side, you have companies that now want to be associated with it. Um we're proud to say that Tyler Reddick, you know, he just announced uh in a his uh Rockstar energy deal with the with the Celsius brand. Um they're they're it's really going well on that front, and it's because of the winning. And and certainly being associated with Jordan's brand and and and my brand is all you know an added bonus to it, but just nothing beats a company tying themselves to a driver that they know is going to go out there and give them the proper exposure and represent them the right way. >> There's there's a big change in the in the C-suite at NASCAR. We have Jim France stepping down, Steve O'Donnell taking the helm. I how will that change the future of NASCAR you think? >> Well, I think that for so long and really ever since kind of the mid-teens 2010s, Jim has been steering the ship. You know, a lot of the the choices that we made were based off of his vision and how he wanted the series to go. Now, I think he's relinquishing those responsibilities now to Steve O'Donnell who has worked himself through really I think two or three decades of working within NASCAR. Now, he's made it all the way to the top to CEO. So, I think it's his vision now to where do you take the sport from this point forward? How do you grow it? That's going to be his biggest challenge is you know, how do you take us from where we are now and growing our audience 50% over the next 5 years or so before our next media rights deal. So, >> How do you do that? How do you how how would you do that? That's a huge increase. >> Well, it is a huge increase, but it's we've been there before, right? I mean, it's it's you know, it's not somewhere we haven't been before. So, it's it's finding new fans. It's embracing your current core fans. And that's a very very tough balance because our core fans demographic is very very different from the new casuals that you want to bring in. So, it's a very tough thing to navigate, but you know, it's going to take a lot of analytics to figure out how you can do that and make everyone happy to um essentially make this sport to where people see it as an experience. When they go to the racetrack, yeah, there's a wreck there's a car race going on, but it's it's an overall weekend experience for your family or your friends or it's a social event. That's, you know, I go to many Charlotte Knights baseball games here in Charlotte. I couldn't tell you one player on a team, but I go there because the social setting, right? Right? And so that you've got to create that kind of atmosphere at the racetrack, and I think that's the challenge that they got going forward. >> I mean, you should Do do you run the F1 playbook? Literally everywhere you look there's something F1 on freaking movie. I mean, there's clothing. I mean, this stuff is everywhere now. >> I I think the commercial side of it, yes. I certainly feel as though when I go to F1 races, the the minute you get to the airport, you know there's an F1 race there. You you know it and you every gas station you stop at, every convenience store, there's some sort of activation going on in the town. NASCAR races, it's not as predominant. I think that that's one place certainly we can improve is the the activation within the town. And so a lot of that comes down to the racetracks themselves. What is their What is their marketing budget? You know, does it need to increase? So, there's lots of different ways to to go about this. I don't know the exactly the perfect way, but it's something that certainly That is something that you can take from F1 and say, we probably could improve in that area. >> I have two fun ones for you, and they come from my friends, and I I think your friends at Yahoo! Sports team. I love that team. I mean, they do amazing things. Two questions. Um one, would you ever do a double? And full and fair disclosure, I didn't know what a double was before. >> Yeah, it's not something that you eat in racing. >> [laughter] >> So, >> no, the double is essentially the Indy 500 and the Coca-Cola 600 uh race. It's on the same day, Memorial Day weekend. Um, and so I I think those years are past me. Uh truthfully, at my age, um I think that uh my best IndyCar possibility years of are far behind. I'm so well trained in NASCAR now, that's I I like to stay in my lane. So, I think there's some young drivers that would be very, very good at it uh and have an opportunity, but it's um it's very hard. I mean, that's 1,100 mi of racing. This is not like running 1,100 mi in your in your vehicle, you know, driving from you know, uh from you know, New York to to Florida. This is [laughter] I mean, you're sustaining heavy G-forces. It's crazy. >> Lots of calorie burn. The heat. >> Um, and so the the double essentially is that happens in one day. So, the Indy 500 typically is run, you know, right around 11:00 to 12:00 in the afternoon. Uh that race will go through 4 hours. Then have the Coca-Cola 600 that goes on in Charlotte. So, you have to then fly an hour. You barely get there in time, but then you can run the 600-mi race NASCAR race. Only a few have done it and and very few have done it successfully to where they've run competitively in both, but I I just think I'm going to be a fan of that from now on. I can't I can't do it. >> Yeah, I have no say in this, of course, but I I don't want you to do it cuz that sounds absolutely >> [laughter] >> That sounds ridiculous. And then lastly, um from the team, how are you coping with a shoulder injury? >> Not been ideal. Um, yeah, I I I tore my rotator cuff probably 3 years ago. I had it repaired 2 years ago. And then I just noticed really in this off-season gosh, it started bothering me again and I and I had it rechecked and they're like, it it never healed. It It's torn just the same as it was 2 years ago. So, now I'm going to have to go through that all over again this off-season. Not ideal. Uh but, I'm just doing everything I can during the week to kind of not do things that that will aggravate it. That way I can use up the strength that I do have in it on Sundays. >> I get it, man. Um look, Danny, thank you for everything you do for the Iowa brand. Um keep crushing it out there in everything you're doing. I would love to stay in touch. Uh the whole team loves you, man. Good to see you. Appreciate it. >> Thank you. >> Appreciate it. All right, and that's it for the latest episode of Power Players with Brian Sazzi. Thank you so much.
